LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only variable definitions (pkgname, pkgver, sources, checksums, etc.) in its top-level scope with no command substitutions, no `eval`, no `curl|bash`, or any other dangerous code. The `pkgver()` function is not defined, so nothing untoward executes during PKGBUILD sourcing. All source URLs point to official Anthropic domains (`code.claude.com`, `downloads.claude.ai`) over HTTPS. The binary sources have pinned SHA-256 checksums. The `package()` function (out of scope for this gate) performs a standard install with a well-documented wrapper script — no malicious behavior. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe.
</details>
<summary>No top-level dangerous code; official sources with pinned checksums.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed cc-legal. Status: SAFE -- Plain documentation file, no security issues.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is clean and follows standard AUR packaging practices:
- **Source URLs**: All downloads point to official Anthropic domains (`downloads.claude.ai`, `code.claude.com`) — no unexpected or third-party hosts.
- **Checksums**: The binary tarballs have pinned SHA-256 sums; the legal text uses `SKIP`, which is normal for a plain-text document and not a concern.
- **Package function**: Only installs the binary into `/opt/claude-code/bin/` and creates a small wrapper script in `/usr/bin/claude` that sets two environment variables (`DISABLE_UPDATES`, `DISABLE_INSTALLATION_CHECKS`) before executing the real binary. This is standard to prevent the application from attempting self-updates or complaining about missing native-installer layout.
- **No dangerous operations**: No `eval`, `base64`, `curl|bash`, obfuscated code, file exfiltration, or backdoor mechanisms.

The file contains no evidence of malicious or supply-chain attack behavior. It is a straightforward package definition for the official Claude Code binary release.
</details>
<summary>Clean AUR package with official sources</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard `nvchecker` configuration for tracking the latest release of `claude-code`. It uses a regex source to check the upstream URL `https://downloads.claude.ai/claude-code-releases/latest`, which is the official download page for Claude Code. The regex `".+"` simply matches any content on that page, which is a common pattern for nvchecker to detect version changes. There is no obfuscation, no code execution, no unexpected network destinations, and no deviation from normal packaging practices. This file is benign.
</details>
<summary>Standard nvchecker config for version tracking.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/claude-code.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-6v5jt9rg...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-6v5jt9rg
Analyzing claude-code AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-6v5jt9rg) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard VCS and HTTP sources that are safely handled during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The `source` array entries are plain URLs pointing to the official vendor domains (code.claude.com and downloads.claude.ai), with filenames explicitly specified. No command substitution, function calls, or executable code appears in the source URLs. The `sha256sums` for non-VCS sources are provided and do not use 'SKIP' except for the legal document, which is not code and serves only for compliance.

The top-level scope contains only standard package metadata and arrays (pkgname, pkgver, depends, etc.), with no immediate command execution (e.g., no backticks, $(...), or !function calls). The `pkgver()` function is absent, so version computation relies solely on the static `pkgver=2.1.183` assignment. There are no side-effecting operations performed at parse time. As such, sourcing the PKGBUILD during source fetching poses no risk of remote code execution or data exfiltration.

While the binary is distributed pre-compiled (common for Bun/Node-based tools), that concern is relevant to later build-stage analysis, not this narrow gate. The two commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only download the declared sources and parse static metadata — both operations are safe here.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous code execution during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous code execution during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, cc-legal...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for the claude-code package, a terminal-based agentic coding tool. It downloads a precompiled binary from the official domain (downloads.claude.ai) for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, with hardcoded, verifiable SHA-256 checksums. The source URLs are directly tied to the declared version and official upstream, which is appropriate. While the license file is fetched over HTTP (from code.claude.com), this is only for documentation and does not impact the binary's integrity.

The package creates a wrapper script to set `DISABLE_UPDATES=1` and `DISABLE_INSTALLATION_CHECKS=1`, which disables the tool's update mechanism and installation health checks. This is a common and acceptable practice in package management to maintain control over software updates and avoid conflicts with the system's packaging policies (e.g., preventing the binary from trying to install itself into ~/.local/bin). No obfuscated code, dangerous runtime downloads, or unauthorized network operations are present.

The file operations are confined to standard packaging locations: `/opt/claude-code/bin/` for the binary and `/usr/bin` for the wrapper symlink. Dependencies and optdepends are reasonable and reflect optional integrations (git, GitHub CLI, etc.). The use of `!strip` is noted due to the binary being a self-contained Bun executable, which is a valid justification. No evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or supply chain tampering is found.
</details>
<summary>
Official binary with proper checksums; no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing cc-legal...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Official binary with proper checksums; no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for cc-legal:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file "cc-legal" is a standard legal and compliance documentation file intended for end users of Claude Code. It contains information about licensing terms, acceptable use policies, authentication methods, compliance frameworks (such as BAA for healthcare), and security reporting procedures. The content is purely informational and does not include executable code, scripts, network calls, or file operations.

There are no signs of malicious intent, obfuscation, or supply-chain attack vectors. Links point to official Anthropic domains (anthropic.com, trust.anthropic.com, hackerone.com/4f1f16ba-...), which are consistent with legitimate support and policy resources. References to OAuth and API keys are descriptive and pertain to proper usage guidelines, not implementation details or credential exfiltration mechanisms.

The file aligns with standard software documentation practices for enterprise and consumer-facing AI services and poses no security risks as distributed.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation only; no executable or malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed cc-legal. Status: SAFE -- Documentation only; no executable or malicious content

Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml...
LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration for nvchecker, a version checking tool commonly used in AUR packages to track upstream releases. The configuration defines a source for "claude-code" using the regex method to extract version information from the URL `https://downloads.claude.ai/claude-code-releases/latest`. The regex pattern ".+" is simplistic and will match any non-zero-length string, which may reduce reliability in version extraction but is not inherently malicious.

This file performs no executable actions, downloads, or system modifications. It merely instructs nvchecker where and how to look for version updates. The URL points to what appears to be the official download domain for Claude Code, aligning with expected upstream behavior. There are no signs of obfuscation, unexpected network destinations, or data exfiltration.

While the overly broad regex may lead to incorrect version detection, this is a configuration weakness rather than a security issue. No malicious or dangerous behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for version checking; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Configuration file for version checking; no malicious content
